ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002678); no specific vegetable dum biryani subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers biryani as a rice-based mixed dish; no vegetable-specific dum biryani code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for vegetable dum biryani or biryani as a food item found in standard SNOMED CT release.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (carrot, peas, potato, cauliflower, beans)
whole spices (bay leaf, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of vegetable dum biryani is approximately 350 g, typically presented as a single plate or portion with rice and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), composite rice-vegetable dish entry; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy basmati rice and mixed vegetable data for micronutrient estimation.
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for vegetable biryani as a composite cooked dish. Macronutrient and key micronutrient values are drawn from this source. Micronutrients not reported by ICMR-NIN (monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, selenium, vitamin D, vitamin E) are set to -1 per sentinel convention. Sodium is estimated from typical restaurant-style preparation using ICMR-NIN spiced rice dish data. Serving size of 350 g is based on standard restaurant portion norms for Indian biryani in Singapore.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — basmati rice-based mixed dish analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for vegetable dum biryani was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 is estimated from the closest analogous food: basmati rice-based mixed dish with vegetables, which has a reported GI of approximately 50–58 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). A midpoint value of 54 is used. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 84.0) / 100 = 45.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 24.0 × 3.5 = 84.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 320 mg per 100 g; a 350 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced salt preparation, as the sodium load from a full restaurant serving can contribute substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (24 g per 100 g; ~84 g per serving) with a moderate GI of 54; glycemic load per serving is high at approximately 45, which can cause significant postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) of vegetable dum biryani, pair it with a low-GI side such as raita or salad, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the high glycemic load of a full serving may challenge glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (approximately 1.8 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium; the vegetable-dominant composition provides dietary fibre and micronutrients that partially offset cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume vegetable dum biryani in moderate portions (200–250 g), preferring preparations made with minimal ghee and requesting lower-sodium seasoning to reduce saturated fat and sodium burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Dum Biryani is a fragrant, layered Indian rice dish prepared using the traditional dum cooking technique, in which long-grain basmati rice and spiced mixed vegetables are sealed together in a heavy pot and slow-cooked over low heat to allow steam to circulate and infuse the rice with aromatic flavours. The dish typically includes vegetables such as carrot, peas, potato, cauliflower, and green beans, combined with whole spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf, as well as ginger, garlic, onion, tomato, yogurt, fresh mint, and coriander. Saffron dissolved in warm milk or water is drizzled over the top layer of rice before sealing, imparting a distinctive golden colour and floral aroma. The result is a moist, fluffy rice dish with tender vegetables, complex spice layering, and a mildly rich flavour from ghee or oil. Nutritionally, vegetable dum biryani provides moderate energy (approximately 148 kcal per 100 g), carbohydrate as the dominant macronutrient (approximately 24 g per 100 g from basmati rice), modest protein (3.5 g per 100 g), and dietary fibre from the vegetable content (approximately 1.8 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low GI), though the glycemic load per standard 350 g restaurant serving is high at approximately 45, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations (approximately 320 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable. It is commonly served at Indian restaurants in Singapore, including establishments such as Zaffron Tandoori, and is a popular choice for both everyday dining and celebratory occasions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in vegetable dum biryani?
Is veg biryani good for diabetics?
Can I eat biryani if I have high blood pressure?
What vegetables are in dum biryani?
How is dum biryani different from regular biryani?
What is the GI of biryani?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for vegetable biryani as a composite cooked dish, which is the highest-priority approved source for Indian cuisine. The match is adapted rather than exact because the specific restaurant preparation (Zaffron Tandoori, Singapore) may differ in ghee quantity, salt level, and vegetable mix from the ICMR-NIN reference preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from basmati rice-based mixed dish data in peer-reviewed literature rather than a direct measurement of this specific dish.
notes: Sentinel values (-1) are used for the following fields where ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy do not report values for this dish type: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg. These are set to -1 per Project Veritas sentinel convention indicating the source does not report these nutrients for this food. Vitamin B12 is -1 as vegetable biryani contains no meat and ICMR-NIN does not report B12 for this dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this dish. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this dish. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this dish. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat fractions are -1 as ICMR-NIN does not disaggregate fat fractions for this composite dish. Trans fat is -1 as ICMR-NIN does not report trans fat for this dish. Sodium of 320 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-style preparation with salt and spice seasoning; home-prepared versions may be lower. The food_name has been normalised to 'Vegetable Dum Biryani' as the canonical English name; 'Zaffron' is a restaurant brand name and is retained in aliases only. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice-based mixed vegetable dish data from Atkinson et al. (2021) Am J Clin Nutr; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.
